coredata.dc:title: A strong blue fluorescent nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) based on sulfur doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Wang, Yongbo
Shi, Huanxian
Yan, Yujia
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: As a rising star in the field of quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing advertence in recent years. In this work, sodium sulfide (Na2S) was used as the sulfur source for the first time to synthesize sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) with stable blue fluorescence by one-step hydrothermal. These synthesized S-CQDs have a uniform size of about 4.2 nm. Compared with CQDs, the prepared S-CQDs showed higher fluorescence intensity. And it also exhibited excitation-independent behavior, relatively high fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 21.1%) and outstanding photobleaching resistance and stability. More importantly, the S-CQDs was used as a novel fluorescent sensor to detect Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Benefitting from above merits, this work provided a new strategy to improve the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and as-prepared S-CQDs showed the potential applications in the detection of heavy metals in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Mercury ion
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
Selectivity and sensitivity of S-CQDs for Hg2+ detection
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476183 and 21676213 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809 ), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China ( 2017JM2026 and 2018JM5020 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the synthesized S-CQDs (a) TEM image (inset is the size distribution histogram) (b) HRTEM images (illustration for partial enlargement) (c) XRD pattern and (d) FTIR spectrum.
XPS (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) S 2p of S-CQDs.

(a) UV absorption of S-CQDs, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, insert are photographs of S-CQDs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) Excitation-independent emission spectra. (c) A possible growth process for S-CQDs.
Fluorescence of CQDs prepared (a)from different sulfur sources, (b) different ratios of citric acid and sodium sulfide, (c) reaction temperatures and (d) reaction time.
(a) Fluorescence spectra under different pH. (b) Resistance to photobleaching performance. (c) The effect of salt on the fluorescence intensity.

Fluorescence quenching effect of biomolecules (a) and metal ions (b), (c) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. (d) The images of the aqueous solution of the S-CQDs and in presence of Hg2+ ions under 365 nm UV light. (e) The dependence of F0/F on the concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 0.5–180 μM. (f) FL intensity of aqueous S-CQDs (black bars),100 μM Hg2+ (red bars) and interference of 100 μM of other metal ion (blue bars).
A possible mechanism of S-CQDs for detecting Hg2+.
Comparison of performance of the S-CQDs sensor with other fluorescent Hg2+ probes.
The recoveries study of Hg2+ in tap water by S-CQDs-based sensor system.

head.ce:title: A strong blue fluorescent nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) based on sulfur doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a rising star in the field of quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing advertence in recent years. In this work, sodium sulfide (Na2S) was used as the sulfur source for the first time to synthesize sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) with stable blue fluorescence by one-step hydrothermal. These synthesized S-CQDs have a uniform size of about 4.2 nm. Compared with CQDs, the prepared S-CQDs showed higher fluorescence intensity. And it also exhibited excitation-independent behavior, relatively high fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 21.1%) and outstanding photobleaching resistance and stability. More importantly, the S-CQDs was used as a novel fluorescent sensor to detect Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Benefitting from above merits, this work provided a new strategy to improve the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and as-prepared S-CQDs showed the potential applications in the detection of heavy metals in the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, CQDs have received growing research interest since they were discovered in 2004 [1], owing to their excellent material properties such as stable photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and good dispersibility in water. Due to the unique properties and advantages of CQDs, it has a wide range of applications in chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine and photocatalysis. For example, metal ion and sulfide anion detection [2,3]; vivo fluorescence imaging [4,5]; cancer diagnosis and treatment in living mice [6]; detection of acetone in human fluid [7], and antibacterial [8]. Recently, several studies have confirmed that doping CQDs with heteroatoms could effectively modulate their band gap and electronic density, enhancing chemical activity, further improving the performance of CQDs in application [9–11]. For instance, nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) showed high performance in electrocatalytic activities and deep-tissue imaging [10,12]. Phosphorus and chlorine or nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as an excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity a probe, for detection iron ion [13], multicolor cellular imaging and hemoglobin probing in human blood [14]. To the best of our knowledge, S-CQDs have relatively rarely been reported, and their unique optical properties are not fully understood. Therefore, it is very attractive to prepare novel S-CQDs by a simple method and explore their potential value on heavy metal detection.

Mercury, as a toxic heavy metal, has attracted increasing concerns for its toxicity effects on the environment and human health [15]. The excessive accumulation of mercury in body can induce irreparable damage to the liver, kidneys and nervous system [16–18]. Up to date, several innovative techniques have been developed for detection and quantification of Hg2+, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic fluorescence spectroscopy, plasma spectroscopy and mass spectrometry [19–22]. However, these detection assays require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures, which greatly limits their practical applications in Hg2+ determination. Recently, the quantum dots fluorescence probe providing a better choice for Hg2+ due to its unique advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, low-cost and easy-to-operate. Nevertheless, most of semiconductor quantum dots fluorescence probe containing heavy metals such as cadmium and lead, resulting in poor biocompatibility, which is not conducive to safe detection in biological systems [23,24]. Therefore, a novel quantum dots fluorescence probe with excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity are urgent developed.

This paper reported a novel high fluorescence property S-CQDs which was firstly synthesized by introducing Na2S as sulfur source and citric acid as carbon precursors through a clean one-step hydrothermal method. To the best of our knowledge, S-CQDs have not been prepared using Na2S as a sulfur source, and Na2S played a very important role in increasing the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The high fluorescence intensity meant higher signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in the fields of heavy metal ion sensors. The synthetic conditions were further optimized by adjusting reaction time, ratio of citric acid to sodium sulfide and reaction temperature to improve the fluorescence intensity. Additionally, the prepared S-CQDs successfully were used to realize the quantitative detection of trace Hg2+ in tap water with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), Zinc acetate, Cadmium chloride and l-cysteine were acquired from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfide (Na2S, 99%), copper chloride and ferric nitrate were purchased commercially from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Sulfuric acid and anhydrous magnesium sulfate were got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Thiourea was derived from Tianjin Yongsheng Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate was purchased from Guangzhou Jinhuada Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Potassium chloride and thioacetamide were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent III. Sodium chloride, ferrous sulfate, aluminum nitrate, barium chloride and urea were supplied by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Chromium nitrate and Lead chloride were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory. Anhydrous calcium chloride was got from Hedong District Hongyan Reagent Factory. Mercury chloride from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Purchasing Supply Station distributor. Ultrapure water was prepared with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). All the chemical reagents used in the experiments were analytical grades without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure information of S-CQDs was taken using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, TF20, JoeL 2100 F) at 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were analyzed by a fluorescence spectrometer (PL, F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) information was detected with a Rigaku D/MAX 3C powder diffractometer. UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra were recorded on the BRUKER VERTEX70 FT-IR spectrometer in a range of 4000–500 cm−1 using KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were studied by using a Kratos AXIS NOVA multi-function imaging electron spectrometer.

The S-CQDs was prepared through a green one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.5 mmol citric acid and 1.5 mmol sodium sulfide were dissolved in 50 ml of water. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 4 h to obtain pale yellow solution. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, a white powder product was obtained by drying at 60 °C.

Quantum yield of S-CQDs was gauged using quinine sulfate (QY 54%) as a standard reference [25]. The QY of S-CQDs was calculated by the following formula.
φ
x
=
φ
s
×
A
s
A
x
×
I
x
I
s
×
η
x
2
η
s
2
. Where
η
,
I
,
A
and
φ
represented the refractive index of the solvent, integral area of fluorescence intensity, absorbance and quantum yield, respectively. The subscript
s
and
x
represented the reference and object, respectively.

The detection of Hg2+ was implemented in aqueous solution at room temperature. A series of Hg2+ aqueous solutions with various concentrations were freshly prepared and added into the aqueous solution containing the same concentration of S-CQDs. Then the quenching effects of Hg2+ on the fluorescence of the S-CQDs were examined. The PL spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm.

The S-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using CA and Na2S. Product was characterized by TEM, XRD, UV–vis, FTIR, XPS and fluorescence spectrometers. It could be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 1 a) that the S-CQDs were spherical and had good dispersibility, and the average size was 4.20 nm. Fig. 1b showed that the S-CQDs had high crystallinity and the lattice parameter was 0.33 nm, which was consistent with the (002) plane of graphite carbon [26]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) showed that there was a broad diffraction peak at 21.6° and was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of the crystalline graphite [27], which is well-agreement with the result of TEM.

Functional groups on the surface of S-CQDs were characterized by FTIR and XPS spectra (Fig. 1d). The broad vibration band at 3336 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group [28,29]. The intense peaks at 1561 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 were assigned to the carbonyl bond and carboxylate radical absorption of the carboxyl group, respectively [30]. The peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 1003 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of carbon-oxygen single bond and the bending vibration of the carbon-hydrogen bond [31]. The peak of 669 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of carbon-sulfur bonds [32] and CA had not peak at 669 cm−1 proved that sulfur was successfully doped into CQDs.

In Fig. 2 a, it can be found that the mainly C, Na, O, S elements were existed in S-CQDs. Besides, the existence of Na signal was attributed to the precursor Na2S. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) can be divided into three peaks located at 284.4, 285.2, and 288 eV, which are attributed to CC (sp2), CO (sp3) and CO (sp2), respectively [33]. The O 1s peaks at 531.8, 532.9 and 533.4 eV shown in Fig. 2c can be assigned to CO, OH and CO, respectively [34,35]. The three peaks (Fig. 2d) at 163.3, 164.4 and 168.8 eV corresponded to the S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 of the CSC covalent bond and C-SOx groups, respectively [36,37]. FTIR and XPS results showed that the surface of S-CQDs had hydroxyl group and carboxylic acid group. These hydrophilic functional groups greatly improved the water solubility of S-CQDs, and it can also be used in biomedical fields by linking biomolecules on hydroxyl and carboxylate radical [38].

The fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of the S-CQDs solution (Fig. 3 a) showed that the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of the S-CQDs were 345 and 445 nm, respectively. The S-CQDs exhibited a UV–vis absorption band centered around 345 nm, which corresponds well to the excitation spectra and should be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of CO [36]. Furthermore, the S-CQDs showed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that the S-CQDs had fewer surface defects, which reduced the non-radiative transition of electrons and caused more electrons to radiate in the form of photons. Fig. 3b showed that the obvious change of the fluorescence intensity but the peak position was almost invariable when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 400 nm, which confirmed S-CQDs were similar to the properties of wide excitation and narrow emission of alloy quantum dots. This excitation-independent fluorescence properties may be related to the uniformity of size and surface state of S-CQDs [39,40]. Based on above results, a possible growth process for the S-CQDs was addressed in Fig. 3c. Firstly, CA and Na2S solution reacted to form deprotonated CA and produced H2S gas, and then deprotonated CA and sulfur ions took place polymerization at high temperature to form crystal nuclei of S-CQDs. Then, the S-CQDs begins to grow from the surface of the nucleus owing to the presence of active functional groups such as carboxylate radical.

Fig. 4 a showed that different heteroatom doping had different effects on the fluorescence of S-CQDs. Among them, Na2S as a sulfur source had a larger fluorescence intensity than undoped CQDs, N-CQDs prepared and S-CQDs prepared by other sulfur sources. And compared with undoped CQDs, the emission peak position was red shifted about 45 nm. The reason may be that the sulfur has a lower electronegativity, which absorbs lower energy light to emit longer wavelength light [41]. The S-CQDs with sodium sulfide as a sulfur doping source had the strongest fluorescence because release rate of sulfur from sodium sulfide was faster than that of other sulfur sources, which was due to the fact that sulfur in sodium sulfide exists as an ionic bond while the other was covalently bond [42]. Different ratio of sulfur doping affected the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs. When the proportion of sulfur was relatively low, all of them participated in deprotonation to form H2S without participating in nucleation. When the ratio of sulfur doping become higher, the repulsive force between sulfur would affect the formation of S-CQDs. Both of these conditions leaded to lower fluorescence intensity, and the experimental results showed that the optimal ratio was 1:3 (Fig. 4b).

The reaction temperature and reaction time will affect the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs. Finally, 200 °C (Fig. 4c) and h (Fig. 4d) are selected as optimum reaction conditions to prepare samples. The QY of the prepared S-CQDs was calculated to be 21.11% by reference to quinine sulfate, which was higher than previously reported S-CQDs [43,44].

It is well known that the stability of CQDs fluorescence is the crucial factor for their practical application. Here, the effects of photobleaching, pH and salt concentration on the fluorescence of S-CQDs were investigated to evaluate the stability of as-prepared S-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the intensity of fluorescence decreased when the S-CQDs were treated with different pH solutions. When the pH was lower than 6, the fluorescence intensity was obviously attenuate. Conversely, when the pH was above 6, the change fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs could be neglected. These results indicated that the as-prepared S-CQDs can act as probe in the neutral or alkaline environments, not in strong acidic environments. The possible reason may attribute to the emission site on the surface of the S-CQDs was protonated under strong acidic conditions, which reduced the radiation transition of the electron, then caused the fluorescence to weaken or even annihilate. However, the emission site on the surface didn't change under neutral or alkaline conditions, thus the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs exhibited no obviously change [40]. Additionally, to investigate the effect of photobleaching the S-CQDs, the S-CQDs was dispersed in pure water and placed under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp for 2 h. As displayed in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity exhibited slightly decrease with the irradiation time prolonged, suggesting the superior resistance to photobleaching of S-CQDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the S-CQDs in different concentrations of Sodium chloride solution was also tested. It can be found that the S-CQDs still had good fluorescence intensity even in the high concentration salt solution (Fig. 5c). The above results suggested that the as-prepared S-CQDs possessed excellent stability and shown a promising prospection for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of S-CQDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR and XPS results demonstrated that the hydroxyl group, carboxylate radical and carbon sulfur bond on the surface of the S-CQDs, which not only enhanced the stability of the S-CQDs, but also were suitable for the detection of metal ion [45]. In order to investigate the selectivity of the S-CQDs, 15 biomolecules and 15 metal ions were chosen as interfering substances under the same concentration (1 mM). As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, excepted for the fluorescence quencher of S-CQDs was induced by Hg2+, the selected interfering materials have no obviously quencher effect on the fluorescence of S-CQDs, indicating the excellent selective and specificity of S-CQDs to Hg2+. There are two possible reasons: on the one hand, since the high affinity between mercury and sulfur, which resulted in the formation of mercury sulfide and affected the electron transfer on the surface of S-CQDs, and increased the probability of non-radiative electron transition and decreased the fluorescence. On the other hand, as shown in Scheme 1 , mercury underwent a complexation reaction with carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S-CQDs, which made the S-CQDs reunite and increase the non-radiative transition of electrons and reduce the fluorescence intensity [46–48].

To study the linear relationship between the concentration of Hg2+ and the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs. The emission spectra of S-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentration were measured. As Fig. 6c depicted, the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs was gradually decaying with the increasing of the concentration of Hg2+ and their pictures under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp were shown in Fig. 6d. They showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.98091) between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.5 μM–180 μM (Fig. 6e). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.222 μM according to the rule of three times the standard deviation. Compared with some other fluorescent Hg2+ probes as shown in Table 1 . It can be seen the S-CQDs Hg2+ probe shows better or comparable performances, including the detection limit, linear range and quantum yield. Therefore, it shown a potential application for detection of Hg2+ in the actual environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real sample contains not only Hg2+, but also other metal ions. Therefore, we explored the quenching effect of the coexistence of Hg2+ with other ions on the S-CQDs fluorescence. (the concentration of Hg2+ and the concentration of other metal ions are 100 μM). As shown in Fig. 6f, the other interfering ions exhibited no effected on the fluorescence intensity of the S-CQDs. Based on the above interference experiments, the as-prepared S-CQDs were used to detection Hg2+ in the tap water. As shown in Table 2 , the results obtained were in good agreement with the spiked values, and the recoveries were 94.38%–102.16%. These results implied that the metal-free S-CQDs shown a potential application for the detection of heavy metal in the environment.

In summary, the S-CQDs with bright blue fluorescence (QYs of 21.11%) were prepared by a simple green one-step hydrothermal method. The S-CQDs displayed non-excitation-dependent characteristics, outstanding photobleaching resistance and excellent stability. Additionally, the prepared S-CQDs were successfully used to realize the quantitative detection of trace Hg2+ in tap water with high sensitivity and selectivity, and the recoveries were 94.38%–102.16%. This work showed a promising prospect for practical application of the S-CQDs in the field of heavy detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476183 and 21676213), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2017JM2026 and 2018JM5020).
